title: Neighborhood water point rules
summary: Rules for a shared water collection or distribution point with hygiene, fairness, priority access, and written records.
content: A water point fails when people cannot trust either cleanliness or fairness. The clean side and dirty side must be separate. Household containers should never touch the shared spout or ladle. The queue needs visible rules, posted hours, a record keeper, and priority access for infants, pregnancy, disability, severe illness, and very old people. If quality is uncertain, the water must be marked untreated. Honesty prevents illness and conflict. The system should be simple enough for tired people: clean containers only, no washing at the source, one direction of movement, no crowding, no hidden changes to ration rules.
materials: Rope or chairs for queue path
steps: Pick a visible place away from toilets, drains, trash, animals, fuel, and standing dirty water.
Assign one filler and one recorder per shift.
Keep household containers below the spout without touching it.
Record shortages, disputes, dirty containers, illness reports, and source changes.
Create priority windows for vulnerable people.
warnings: Dirty containers can contaminate the whole point.
Do not let aggressive or intoxicated people control the queue.
common_mistakes: Using one record keeper until exhaustion causes errors.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Neighborhood water point rules with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Neighborhood water point rules if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Neighborhood water point rules and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Neighborhood water point rules or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Neighborhood water point rules are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Neighborhood water point rules”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Neighborhood water point rules as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Neighborhood water point rules when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Neighborhood water point rules.
short_term: For the first hours of Neighborhood water point rules, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Neighborhood water point rules remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Neighborhood water point rules fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Neighborhood water point rules to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
